items.correct: Removing an unpleasant stimulus to INCREASE a behaviour.
items.misconception: Punishing a behaviour to decrease it.
items.random_wrong: Giving a reward to decrease behaviour.
items.why: Students confuse negative reinforcement with punishment. Both involve something unpleasant, but reinforcement (positive or negative) always INCREASES behaviour; punishment decreases it.

items.q: How do SSRIs help with OCD?
items.correct: They prevent reabsorption of serotonin, increasing its availability in the synapse.
items.misconception: They directly produce more serotonin in the brain.
items.why: Students think SSRIs create serotonin. They actually inhibit REUPTAKE - serotonin already released stays in the synapse longer, increasing post-synaptic stimulation.

items.q: Asch's line study primarily demonstrates which type of conformity?
items.correct: Normative social influence (compliance to fit in).
items.misconception: Informational social influence (believing others know better).
items.why: Students assume participants genuinely believed the wrong line was correct. Asch's debriefs showed they knew it was wrong but conformed publicly to avoid rejection - this is normative, not informational.
It's Brain making sense of input. “Pure data” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Past knowledge. “Only the eyes” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An illusion shows 2 equal lines that look different. % different they really are?
items.why: Exact answer: 0. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
